text: Depth-First Search Prof. Timothy Sun Depth-ﬁrst search DFS is especially useful because of the structure given by the pre-order and post-order traversals through the graph. Pre-order traversal UK line of succession Pre-order traversal UK line of succession Post-order traversal Celebrity Jeopardy! schedule Post-order traversal Celebrity Jeopardy! schedule Depth-ﬁrst search DFS is especially useful because of the structure given by the pre-order and post-order traversals through the graph. Just like connected components, we try doing DFS on every vertex, so every vertex is visited at some point. A “clock” keeps track of when a vertex is on the recursion stack: ● New: clock < pre(v) ● Active: pre(v) < clock < post(v) ● Finished: post(v) < clock Depth-ﬁrst search A “clock” keeps track of when a vertex is on the recursion stack: ● New: clock < pre(v) ● Active: pre(v) < clock < post(v) ● Finished: post(v) < clock All DFSes in this class will be done in alphabetical order. Depth-ﬁrst search Depth-ﬁrst search Depth-ﬁrst search back edge forward edge tree edge cross edge Depth-ﬁrst search back forward tree cross Depth-ﬁrst search intervals are nesting, or disjoint back forward tree cross Parentheses Theorem back forward tree cross Back edge (e.g., u → s) ● [u] ⊆ [s] ○ pre(s) < pre(u) ○ post(s) > post(u) Parentheses Theorem back forward tree cross Tree edge (e.g., s → t) ● [t] ⊆ [s] ○ pre(s) < pre(t) ○ post(s) > post(t) ● parent(t) = s Parentheses Theorem back forward tree cross Forward edge (e.g., t → v) ● [v] ⊆ [t] ○ pre(t) < pre(v) ○ post(t) > post(v) ● parent(v) ≠ t Parentheses Theorem back forward tree cross Cross edge (e.g., w → v) ● [v] before [w] ○ pre(v) < post(v) < pre(w) < post(w) Parentheses Theorem back forward tree cross back [a] ⊆ [b] post(a) < post(b) tree/forward [b] ⊆ [a] pre(a) < pre(b) cross [b] before [a] otherwise For a directed edge (a→b), Parentheses Theorem back forward tree cross What about the fourth possibility? ● a→b: [a] before [b] Parentheses Theorem back forward tree cross What about the fourth possibility? ● a→b: [a] before [b] Not possible! Undirected DFS only tree and back edges Some more examples Some more examples Some more examples Some more examples Some more examples Some more examples Finding a directed cycle Claim: a directed cycle exists iff there is a back edge back forward tree cross Finding a directed cycle How does a DFS interact with a cycle? Finding a directed cycle How does a DFS interact with a cycle? 1. Visits some vertex v ﬁrst Finding a directed cycle How does a DFS interact with a cycle? 1. Visits some vertex v ﬁrst 2. Eventually reaches predecessor u Finding a directed cycle How does a DFS interact with a cycle? 1. Visits some vertex v ﬁrst 2. Eventually reaches predecessor u 3. Then u→v is a backedge. Finding a directed cycle Cycle-ﬁnding algorithm: ● Do DFS. ● Check for back edges. Finding a directed cycle Cycle-ﬁnding algorithm: ● Do DFS. O(|V|+|E|) ● Check for back edges. O(|E|) In total: O(|V|+|E|) Finding a directed cycle Cycle-ﬁnding algorithm: ● Do DFS. O(|V|+|E|) ● Check for back edges. O(|E|) In total: O(|V|+|E|) Finding a directed cycle Cycle-ﬁnding algorithm: ● Do DFS. O(|V|+|E|) ● Check for back edges. O(|E|) In total: O(|V|+|E|) Can also modify DFSAll to ﬁnd a cycle “along the way.” A directed acyclic graph (DAG) has no directed cycles DAGs and topological sorts DAGs and topological sorts A directed acyclic graph (DAG) has no directed cycles A topological sort is an ordering of the vertices where all edges point “to the right”: I, B, E, H, G, C, F, A, D DAGs and topological sorts Claim: A graph is a DAG iff it has a topological sort DAGs and topological sorts Claim: A graph is a DAG iff it has a topological sort Easier direction: topological sort → DAG DAGs and topological sorts Claim: A graph is a DAG iff it has a topological sort Easier direction: topological sort → DAG ● A cycle would have a left-pointing edge DAGs and topological sorts Claim: A graph is a DAG iff it has a topological sort Easier direction: topological sort → DAG ● A cycle would have a left-pointing edge Harder direction: DAG → topological sort Every DAG has a source and a sink. ● Source: indegree 0 (B, I) ● Sink: outdegree 0 (D, F) Sources and sinks Sources and sinks Every DAG has a source and a sink. ● Source: indegree 0 (B, I) ● Sink: outdegree 0 (D, F) Proof sketch: keep walking… Sources and sinks Every DAG has a source and a sink. ● Source: indegree 0 (B, I) ● Sink: outdegree 0 (D, F) Proof sketch: keep walking… Topological sort: remove a source and recurse Information from a DFS Recall: ● (u→v) back edge → post(u) < post(v) Information from a DFS Recall: ● (u→v) back edge → post(u) < post(v) In a DAG, post(u) > post(v) for every edge Topological sorting Postorder traversal is a topological sort, but backwards Topological sort algorithm: 1. Do DFS. 2. Reverse postorder traversal. Topological sort from DFS Topological sort from DFS Topological sort from DFS Topological sort from DFS Topological sorting Postorder traversal is a topological sort, but backwards Topological sort algorithm: 1. Do DFS. O(|V| + |E|) 2. Reverse postorder traversal. O(|V|) Total runtime: O(|V| + |E|)

pages.text: Depth-First Search Prof. Timothy Sun
Depth-ﬁrst search DFS is especially useful because of the structure given by the pre-order and post-order traversals through the graph.
Pre-order traversal UK line of succession
Pre-order traversal UK line of succession
Post-order traversal Celebrity Jeopardy! schedule
Post-order traversal Celebrity Jeopardy! schedule

Depth-ﬁrst search DFS is especially useful because of the structure given by the pre-order and post-order traversals through the graph. Just like connected components, we try doing DFS on every vertex, so every vertex is visited at some point.
A “clock” keeps track of when a vertex is on the recursion stack: ● New: clock < pre(v) ● Active: pre(v) < clock < post(v) ● Finished: post(v) < clock Depth-ﬁrst search

A “clock” keeps track of when a vertex is on the recursion stack: ● New: clock < pre(v) ● Active: pre(v) < clock < post(v) ● Finished: post(v) < clock All DFSes in this class will be done in alphabetical order. Depth-ﬁrst search
Depth-ﬁrst search
Depth-ﬁrst search back edge forward edge tree edge cross edge
Depth-ﬁrst search back forward tree cross
Depth-ﬁrst search intervals are nesting, or disjoint back forward tree cross
Parentheses Theorem back forward tree cross Back edge (e.g., u → s) ● [u] ⊆ [s] ○ pre(s) < pre(u) ○ post(s) > post(u)
Parentheses Theorem back forward tree cross Tree edge (e.g., s → t) ● [t] ⊆ [s] ○ pre(s) < pre(t) ○ post(s) > post(t) ● parent(t) = s
Parentheses Theorem back forward tree cross Forward edge (e.g., t → v) ● [v] ⊆ [t] ○ pre(t) < pre(v) ○ post(t) > post(v) ● parent(v) ≠ t
Parentheses Theorem back forward tree cross Cross edge (e.g., w → v) ● [v] before [w] ○ pre(v) < post(v) < pre(w) < post(w)
Parentheses Theorem back forward tree cross back [a] ⊆ [b] post(a) < post(b) tree/forward [b] ⊆ [a] pre(a) < pre(b) cross [b] before [a] otherwise For a directed edge (a→b),
Parentheses Theorem back forward tree cross What about the fourth possibility? ● a→b: [a] before [b]
Parentheses Theorem back forward tree cross What about the fourth possibility? ● a→b: [a] before [b] Not possible!

Finding a directed cycle How does a DFS interact with a cycle?
Finding a directed cycle How does a DFS interact with a cycle? 1. Visits some vertex v ﬁrst
Finding a directed cycle How does a DFS interact with a cycle? 1. Visits some vertex v ﬁrst 2. Eventually reaches predecessor u
Finding a directed cycle How does a DFS interact with a cycle? 1. Visits some vertex v ﬁrst 2. Eventually reaches predecessor u 3. Then u→v is a backedge.
Finding a directed cycle Cycle-ﬁnding algorithm: ● Do DFS. ● Check for back edges.
Finding a directed cycle Cycle-ﬁnding algorithm: ● Do DFS. O(|V|+|E|) ● Check for back edges. O(|E|) In total: O(|V|+|E|)
Finding a directed cycle Cycle-ﬁnding algorithm: ● Do DFS. O(|V|+|E|) ● Check for back edges. O(|E|) In total: O(|V|+|E|)
Finding a directed cycle Cycle-ﬁnding algorithm: ● Do DFS. O(|V|+|E|) ● Check for back edges. O(|E|) In total: O(|V|+|E|) Can also modify DFSAll to ﬁnd a cycle “along the way.”
A directed acyclic graph (DAG) has no directed cycles DAGs and topological sorts
DAGs and topological sorts A directed acyclic graph (DAG) has no directed cycles A topological sort is an ordering of the vertices where all edges point “to the right”: I, B, E, H, G, C, F, A, D
DAGs and topological sorts Claim: A graph is a DAG iff it has a topological sort
DAGs and topological sorts Claim: A graph is a DAG iff it has a topological sort Easier direction: topological sort → DAG
DAGs and topological sorts Claim: A graph is a DAG iff it has a topological sort Easier direction: topological sort → DAG ● A cycle would have a left-pointing edge

DAGs and topological sorts Claim: A graph is a DAG iff it has a topological sort Easier direction: topological sort → DAG ● A cycle would have a left-pointing edge Harder direction: DAG → topological sort
Every DAG has a source and a sink. ● Source: indegree 0 (B, I) ● Sink: outdegree 0 (D, F) Sources and sinks
Sources and sinks Every DAG has a source and a sink. ● Source: indegree 0 (B, I) ● Sink: outdegree 0 (D, F) Proof sketch: keep walking…
Sources and sinks Every DAG has a source and a sink. ● Source: indegree 0 (B, I) ● Sink: outdegree 0 (D, F) Proof sketch: keep walking… Topological sort: remove a source and recurse
Information from a DFS Recall: ● (u→v) back edge → post(u) < post(v)
Information from a DFS Recall: ● (u→v) back edge → post(u) < post(v) In a DAG, post(u) > post(v) for every edge
Topological sorting Postorder traversal is a topological sort, but backwards Topological sort algorithm: 1. Do DFS. 2. Reverse postorder traversal.

Topological sorting Postorder traversal is a topological sort, but backwards Topological sort algorithm: 1. Do DFS. O(|V| + |E|) 2. Reverse postorder traversal. O(|V|) Total runtime: O(|V| + |E|)
